What is DevOps?: DevOps is a culture and set of practices that combines software development and IT operations to shorten the delivery lifecycle, using automation, CI/CD, and shared ownership so teams can build, test, and release software faster and more reliably.
What is a DevOps Engineer?: A DevOps Engineer is someone who builds and operates the automation, infrastructure, and pipelines that let development and operations work as one flow - owning CI/CD, IaC, monitoring, and reliability rather than just writing application features or just keeping servers up.
What is the difference between DevOps an: Agile is a software development methodology focused on iterative planning and delivering working software in short cycles with close customer collaboration; DevOps extends that further downstream into deployment and operations, focused on automating build/test/release/monitor so those iterations actually reach production quickly and reliably - Agile answers 'how do we plan and build,' DevOps answers 'how do we ship and run it.'
What is Configuration Management?: Configuration Management is the practice of defining and enforcing a consistent, known state for servers and infrastructure (installed packages, config files, running services) through automation tools like Ansible, Puppet, or Chef, instead of manually configuring each machine and letting them drift apart over time.
What is CI/CD?: CI/CD stands for Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery/Deployment - CI automatically builds and tests every code change as it's merged, while CD automatically packages and ships that validated code to staging or production.
What is the difference between Continuou: Continuous Delivery means every change that passes the pipeline is automatically packaged and ready to release, but a human still triggers the actual production release; Continuous Deployment goes one step further and releases every passing change to production automatically with no manual gate.
What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?: Infrastructure as Code is the practice of defining and managing infrastructure (servers, networks, databases) through versioned, declarative configuration files instead of manual console clicks, making infrastructure repeatable, reviewable, and auditable.
What is a virtual machine (VM), and how: A VM virtualizes hardware and runs a full guest operating system on top of a hypervisor, giving strong isolation but at the cost of more memory/CPU overhead and slower startup; a container virtualizes at the OS level, sharing the host kernel and packaging only the application and its dependencies, making it far lighter and faster to start than a VM.
What is a container?: A container is a lightweight, isolated unit that packages an application with all its dependencies and runs using the host OS's kernel, giving process-level isolation without the overhead of a full virtual machine.
What is the difference between a Docker: A Docker image is the read-only, immutable template (filesystem layers plus metadata) built from a Dockerfile; a container is a running (or stopped) instance of that image with its own writable layer, network, and process space.
What is a Dockerfile?: A Dockerfile is a text file with a sequence of instructions (FROM, COPY, RUN, CMD, etc.) that Docker reads to build a container image layer by layer.
How does a multi-stage Docker build work: A multi-stage build uses multiple FROM statements in one Dockerfile, where an early stage (e.g. a full SDK image) compiles or builds the application, and a later, much smaller final stage (e.g. a minimal or distroless base) copies over only the compiled artifact using COPY --from=<stage>. It fixes bloated production images - build tools, compilers, and source code needed only to produce the artifact never end up in the final image, drastically cutting image size and attack surface.
What is Kubernetes?: Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates deploying, scaling, healing, and networking containerized applications across a cluster of machines.
What is a Pod in Kubernetes?: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes - one or more tightly coupled containers that share the same network namespace, IP address, and storage volumes, and are always scheduled together on the same node.
What is a Node in Kubernetes?: A Node is a single worker machine (physical or virtual) in a Kubernetes cluster that runs Pods, managed by the kubelet agent and container runtime, with cluster-wide scheduling handled by the control plane.
What is a Kubernetes namespace?: A namespace is a virtual partition within a Kubernetes cluster that scopes resource names and access controls, letting multiple teams or environments share one cluster without naming collisions.
What is a Kubernetes Deployment?: A Deployment is a Kubernetes controller that manages a ReplicaSet of Pods, handling declarative rollouts, rolling updates, and rollbacks so you describe the desired state and Kubernetes reconciles the actual state to match.
What is a Kubernetes Service?: A Service is a stable network endpoint (a fixed IP and DNS name) that load-balances traffic to a dynamic set of Pods matching a label selector, so clients don't need to track individual Pod IPs that change as Pods are created or destroyed.
How do you upgrade/update a Kubernetes c: You upgrade the control plane first (in GKE this is largely managed - you trigger or schedule it via gcloud/console), then upgrade node pools afterward, typically one pool at a time using a surge or rolling upgrade strategy so new nodes with the newer kubelet version are added and old nodes are cordoned, drained, and removed gradually. Always check the Kubernetes version skew policy (control plane can be at most a few minor versions ahead of kubelets) and review deprecated/removed API versions in the target release before upgrading, since workloads using removed APIs will break.
How does the GKE API server (control pla: In GKE, Google manages the control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager) on Google-owned infrastructure and exposes the API server through a public or private endpoint (configurable) fronted by a load balancer with TLS; kubectl and other clients authenticate via IAM-backed credentials/tokens and reach the API server over that endpoint, while node-to-control-plane and pod-to-API-server traffic within the cluster is routed over the VPC (using Konnectivity/tunnels for private clusters where nodes have no public IPs).
What is etcd, and what happens to the cl: etcd is the distributed, consistent key-value store that holds the entire Kubernetes cluster's state - every object (Pods, Deployments, Secrets, ConfigMaps) is persisted there, and the API server reads/writes through it. If etcd goes down, the cluster effectively becomes read-only or fully unavailable for changes: existing Pods keep running (kubelets continue managing already-scheduled workloads), but no new scheduling, updates, or API writes can succeed until etcd recovers, which is why etcd is run as a highly-available odd-numbered cluster (typically 3 or 5 members) with regular snapshots for disaster recovery.
What does OOMKilled mean, and how do you: OOMKilled means a container was terminated by the kernel's OOM (Out Of Memory) killer because it exceeded its configured memory limit (or the node ran out of memory). Fix it by profiling actual memory usage and raising the container's memory limit to a realistic value, fixing a memory leak in the application if usage grows unbounded over time, or reducing the workload's memory footprint (batch size, cache size) - simply raising the limit without checking for a leak just delays the same crash at a higher memory ceiling.
What happens when a Kubernetes node fail: The kubelet on that node stops sending heartbeats to the API server, and after the node-monitor-grace-period (default 40s) the node is marked NotReady; after a further pod-eviction-timeout (default 5 minutes) the control plane considers the node's Pods unreachable and, for Pods owned by a Deployment/ReplicaSet, reschedules replacements onto healthy nodes to restore the desired replica count - Pods not managed by a controller (bare Pods) are not automatically rescheduled.
What happens to the Horizontal Pod Autos: The HPA controller can no longer fetch current CPU/memory (or custom) metrics, so it stops making any new scaling decisions - up or down - and holds at the last known replica count, logging a 'FailedGetResourceMetric' or similar event, until the metrics server recovers and fresh metrics are available again. This means a traffic spike during a metrics-server outage gets no autoscaling response at all, which is why metrics-server itself needs to be monitored and highly available.
What are taints and tolerations in Kuber: A taint is applied to a node to repel Pods from scheduling there unless they explicitly tolerate it, and a toleration is applied to a Pod to allow (not force) scheduling onto nodes with a matching taint - together they let you reserve nodes for specific workloads (e.g. GPU or dedicated node pools).
How is a Kubernetes cluster monitored -: The standard stack is Prometheus scraping metrics from kube-state-metrics (object-level state like Pod status and Deployment replicas), node-exporter (host-level CPU/memory/disk), and application /metrics endpoints, with Grafana for dashboards and Alertmanager for routing alerts. On GKE, Cloud Monitoring/Cloud Logging provide this out of the box with Managed Service for Prometheus as an option. Layer in centralized logging (Cloud Logging or the ELK/EFK stack) and distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) so you can correlate metrics, logs, and traces when diagnosing an issue rather than relying on any single signal alone.
Kubernetes shows a Deployment as healthy: This gap almost always means Kubernetes' liveness/readiness checks are too shallow to catch the real problem, so start by checking what those probes actually test (often just 'process is up', not 'app is functionally correct'). Then check application-level signals Kubernetes doesn't see: error rate and latency in app logs/traces, dependency health (database, downstream API, cache) that the app can't self-report through a basic health check, and whether the alert is a false positive from a misconfigured Grafana query or threshold. Fix at two levels - make the liveness/readiness probes actually reflect real health (a /ready endpoint that checks DB connectivity, not just 'server started'), and fix the root cause found in logs/traces (bad config, downstream outage, resource starvation not yet triggering a Pod restart). If the app is silently degraded but not crashing, add or tighten SLO-based alerts (latency/error-rate) so Kubernetes' pass/fail Pod status stops being the only signal you trust.
What is a Helm chart?: A Helm chart is a packaged, templated collection of Kubernetes manifests (Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, etc.) that can be parameterized with values and installed, upgraded, or rolled back as a single versioned release.
What is Terraform?: Terraform is an open-source Infrastructure as Code tool that lets you define cloud and on-prem resources in a declarative HCL configuration, then plans and applies the changes needed to make real infrastructure match that configuration.
What is a Terraform state file?: The state file is Terraform's record of the real-world resources it currently manages and their attributes, used to compute the diff between desired configuration and actual infrastructure on every plan/apply.
What is a Terraform module?: A Terraform module is a reusable, self-contained group of resource definitions with input variables and outputs, called from other configurations to avoid duplicating the same infrastructure pattern repeatedly.
What is Ansible?: Ansible is an agentless, push-based configuration management and automation tool that connects over SSH to apply YAML-defined playbooks, configuring servers or orchestrating multi-step deployments without installing any software on the target hosts.
How does Ansible help in DevOps and infr: Ansible replaces manual, error-prone server setup with repeatable playbooks - the same playbook configures 1 server or 1,000 identically, eliminates configuration drift by re-applying desired state, and turns operational runbooks (patching, deployments, failover) into version-controlled, peer-reviewed code. In a DevOps pipeline it's commonly used to provision and configure servers after Terraform creates them, orchestrate zero-downtime rolling deployments, and enforce security baselines consistently across an entire fleet.
What is an Ansible playbook?: A playbook is a YAML file describing an ordered list of plays and tasks - each naming an Ansible module and its parameters - that define the desired state to apply to a group of hosts from the inventory.
What is a role in Ansible?: A role is a standardized directory structure that packages related tasks, variables, templates, handlers, and files for one piece of functionality (like 'install nginx'), so it can be reused across multiple playbooks instead of duplicating the same tasks.
What is a task in Ansible?: A task is the smallest unit of work in a playbook - a single call to an Ansible module with specific parameters, like installing a package or copying a file, executed in order against the targeted hosts.
What is an ad-hoc command in Ansible?: An ad-hoc command is a one-off Ansible module call run directly from the command line (e.g. `ansible all -m ping` or `ansible webservers -m service -a "name=nginx state=restarted"`) for quick, single-purpose actions that don't need a full playbook.
What is the Ansible template module?: The template module copies a Jinja2 template file to a remote host, rendering any variables and expressions in it first, so you can generate host-specific config files (like nginx.conf) from a single templated source instead of maintaining a separate static file per host.
What is idempotency?: Idempotency means running an operation multiple times produces the same end result as running it once - re-applying a Terraform plan or Ansible playbook when nothing has changed should make no further changes, which is what makes automation safe to re-run.
How would you optimize a Google Cloud Ru: Set a minimum instance count so warm instances are always ready instead of scaling to zero, reduce container image size and simplify startup (lazy-load heavy dependencies, avoid unnecessary initialization work at boot), enable CPU always-allocated if the workload needs background processing, tune concurrency so fewer cold instances are needed under load, and use a smaller/leaner base image (distroless or slim) so the container starts faster. If cold starts are still an issue for latency-sensitive paths, keep min-instances above zero permanently for that service.
What is Artifact Registry?: Artifact Registry is Google Cloud's managed service for storing and managing container images, language packages (npm, Maven, Python), and other build artifacts, with per-repository IAM, vulnerability scanning, and regional replication - it's the successor to the older Container Registry.
What is Cloud Build, and what does a typ: Cloud Build is Google Cloud's managed CI/CD service that executes build steps defined in a cloudbuild.yaml (or Dockerfile) as a sequence of containerized steps. A typical workflow: a trigger fires on a git push, Cloud Build checks out the code, builds a container image, runs tests and vulnerability scans, pushes the image to Artifact Registry, then deploys it (to Cloud Run, GKE, or via Cloud Deploy) - each step running in its own container for isolation.
What is Cloud Deploy?: Cloud Deploy is Google Cloud's managed continuous delivery service for orchestrating progressive, multi-stage rollouts (dev to staging to prod) to GKE or Cloud Run, handling promotion approvals, canary/rolling delivery strategies, and rollback, on top of artifacts already built (typically by Cloud Build).
How does Cloud Armor work to protect a s: Cloud Armor is a Google Cloud WAF and DDoS protection service that attaches to an external HTTP(S) Load Balancer and evaluates incoming requests against configured rules - IP allow/deny lists, geo-based restrictions, preconfigured WAF rules (SQLi, XSS), and rate limiting - blocking or throttling malicious traffic before it ever reaches your backend.
How does Istio work as a service mesh -: Istio injects a sidecar Envoy proxy into every Pod, and all inbound/outbound traffic for that Pod is transparently routed through its sidecar rather than going directly between application containers. The control plane (istiod) pushes routing rules, mTLS certificates, and policy configuration to every sidecar, so when Service A calls Service B, the request actually goes app-A to sidecar-A to sidecar-B to app-B, with the sidecars handling encryption, retries, load balancing, and telemetry along the way - all without the application code being aware of it.
What is a load balancer?: A load balancer distributes incoming network traffic across multiple backend servers or instances, improving availability and throughput and removing any single instance as a point of failure.
What is DNS?: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (like example.com) into the IP addresses machines use to route network traffic to the correct server.
What is a firewall?: A firewall is a network security control that inspects and filters incoming and outgoing traffic based on rules (source/destination IP, port, protocol), blocking traffic that doesn't match an allowed rule.
What is a VPC?: A VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is an isolated, logically-defined private network within a cloud provider where you control IP ranges, subnets, routing, and connectivity for your resources.
What is a subnet?: A subnet is a smaller, segmented range of IP addresses carved out of a VPC's larger address space, typically used to separate resources by tier (public-facing vs. private) or by availability zone.
In simple terms, how does network traffi: A client request hits an external Load Balancer (provisioned via a Kubernetes Ingress or Service of type LoadBalancer), which forwards it to a node in the cluster; kube-proxy's routing rules on that node (or the LB directly, with container-native load balancing) send the request to a Pod matching the Service's label selector, using the Service's stable virtual IP as the indirection layer so the client never needs to know individual Pod IPs, which change as Pods are created and destroyed.
What is VPC Peering, and how does it dif: VPC Peering connects two VPC networks (in the same or different projects/organizations) directly over Google's internal network using private IPs, with no gateway or extra latency, but it doesn't transitively route through a third peered network. Cloud Interconnect and Partner Interconnect instead connect your on-premises network to Google Cloud - Dedicated Interconnect is a direct physical connection into a Google colocation facility, while Partner Interconnect goes through a supported service provider for locations without direct physical access - both used for hybrid cloud connectivity rather than VPC-to-VPC peering.
What is IAM?: IAM (Identity and Access Management) is the system that controls who (users, service accounts, applications) can do what (which actions) on which resources, typically through roles and policies that follow least-privilege principles.
What is RBAC?: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) grants permissions by assigning users or service accounts to roles that bundle a defined set of allowed actions, rather than assigning permissions to individuals one by one.
What is the difference between a Role an: A Role grants permissions scoped to a single namespace and is paired with a RoleBinding; a ClusterRole grants permissions cluster-wide (or can be bound within a single namespace via a RoleBinding too), and is paired with a ClusterRoleBinding for cluster-wide access - use ClusterRole for cluster-scoped resources (like nodes) or when the same permission set needs to be reused across many namespaces.
What is the difference between a RoleBin: A RoleBinding grants the permissions in a Role (or ClusterRole) to a user/group/service account within one specific namespace only; a ClusterRoleBinding grants the permissions in a ClusterRole across the entire cluster, all namespaces included - so the binding controls the scope of access even when it references the same underlying role.
What is GKE Workload Identity?: Workload Identity lets a Kubernetes ServiceAccount in GKE impersonate a Google Cloud IAM service account without storing any exported JSON key on the cluster - pods use their Kubernetes ServiceAccount to obtain short-lived Google credentials automatically, which is the recommended, more secure alternative to mounting service account key files as secrets.
What is the difference between a statefu: A stateless application doesn't retain data between requests - any instance can handle any request, making it trivial to scale horizontally and replace; a stateful application persists data (in memory or on disk) tied to a specific instance, like a database, requiring stable identity, persistent storage, and careful handling during scaling or failover (which is exactly what Kubernetes StatefulSets are designed for).
What is a secret (in the context of Kube: A secret is a piece of sensitive data - passwords, API keys, TLS certificates, tokens - stored and transmitted separately from application code/config, typically encrypted at rest and access-controlled, rather than hardcoded or committed to source control.
What is autoscaling?: Autoscaling automatically adjusts the number of running instances or Pods up or down based on real-time metrics like CPU, memory, or request rate, so capacity matches actual demand without manual intervention.
What is the difference between horizonta: Horizontal scaling adds more instances/Pods running in parallel to handle more load; vertical scaling increases the resources (CPU, memory) of an existing instance - horizontal scaling is generally preferred in cloud-native systems since it avoids a hard ceiling and single points of failure.
What is a reverse proxy?: A reverse proxy sits in front of backend servers and forwards client requests to them, commonly used for load balancing, TLS termination, caching, and hiding backend topology from clients.
What is TLS/SSL?: TLS (the successor to SSL) is a cryptographic protocol that encrypts data in transit between a client and server and verifies server identity via certificates, which is what makes HTTPS secure.
What is a container registry?: A container registry is a storage and distribution service for container images (like Docker Hub, Artifact Registry, or ECR) where images are pushed after a build and pulled from when deploying.
What is a rollback?: A rollback reverts a deployment to its previous known-good version after a bad release, typically by redeploying the prior image/version or, in Kubernetes, using the Deployment's revision history.
What is a blue-green deployment?: Blue-green deployment runs two identical production environments (blue = current, green = new); traffic is switched all at once from blue to green after the new version is verified, allowing an instant rollback by switching traffic back.
What is a canary deployment?: A canary deployment gradually shifts a small percentage of production traffic to a new version first, monitoring for errors before progressively increasing traffic, limiting the blast radius of a bad release.
What is a service mesh?: A service mesh is an infrastructure layer (like Istio) that manages service-to-service communication - routing, retries, mTLS encryption, observability - via sidecar proxies, without requiring changes to application code.
What is observability?: Observability is the ability to understand a system's internal state from its external outputs - metrics, logs, and traces - so you can diagnose novel problems, not just the ones you predicted and built dashboards for in advance.
What is the difference between SLA, SLI,: An SLI (Service Level Indicator) is a measured metric like latency or availability; an SLO (Service Level Objective) is the internal target for that metric (e.g. 99.9% uptime); an SLA (Service Level Agreement) is the external, often contractual, commitment to customers, usually with penalties if missed.
What is an error budget?: An error budget is the allowed amount of unreliability (100% minus the SLO) a service can spend before teams must pause feature releases and prioritize reliability work instead.
What is a runbook?: A runbook is a documented, step-by-step procedure for diagnosing and resolving a specific operational issue, used during incidents so response doesn't depend on one person's memory.
What is a postmortem?: A postmortem (or incident review) is a blameless, written analysis of an incident covering what happened, its impact, root cause, and concrete follow-up action items to prevent recurrence.
What is version control?: Version control is a system (like Git) that tracks changes to files over time, letting multiple people collaborate, review history, branch, and revert changes safely.
What is a Git branch?: A Git branch is an independent line of development - a movable pointer to a commit - that lets you work on changes in isolation from the main codebase until they're ready to merge.
What is a Git merge conflict?: A merge conflict happens when Git can't automatically reconcile changes because two branches modified the same lines of a file differently, requiring a human to manually choose or combine the correct result.
What is git stash?: git stash temporarily saves your uncommitted changes (staged and unstaged) onto a stack and reverts your working directory to a clean state, so you can switch branches or pull updates and later reapply those changes with git stash pop.
What is the difference between git fetch: git fetch downloads new commits and branches from the remote into your local repository without touching your working directory or current branch; git pull does a fetch and then immediately merges (or rebases) the fetched changes into your current branch - fetch is the safe, look-before-you-merge option.
What is git cherry-pick used for?: git cherry-pick applies a specific commit from one branch onto another branch without merging the entire branch history, useful for porting a single bug fix to a release branch without pulling in unrelated changes.
What is git squash?: Squashing combines multiple sequential commits into a single commit (typically via interactive rebase, git rebase -i), used to clean up a messy work-in-progress history into one meaningful commit before merging.
What is Jenkins?: Jenkins is a widely-used open-source, self-hosted automation server for building CI/CD pipelines - it runs builds, tests, and deployments based on triggers (commits, schedules, webhooks), extended through a large plugin ecosystem.
What is Puppet, and how does it compare: Puppet is a configuration management tool that uses a declarative, agent-based, pull model - agents installed on each managed node periodically check in with a central Puppet master and pull their configuration - whereas Ansible is agentless and push-based over SSH; Puppet suits large, stable fleets needing continuous self-correcting enforcement, while Ansible is simpler to start with since there's no agent to install or maintain.
What is the difference between HTTP and: HTTP transmits data in plain text between client and server; HTTPS is HTTP layered over TLS/SSL, which encrypts that traffic and verifies the server's identity via a certificate, protecting against eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks.
What is a REST API?: A REST API is a web service interface that uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) on resource-based URLs to let clients create, read, update, and delete data over HTTP.
What is YAML used for?: YAML is a human-readable data serialization format used to write structured configuration - Kubernetes manifests, Ansible playbooks, CI/CD pipeline definitions - because its indentation-based syntax is easier to read and write than JSON or XML.
What is a cron job?: A cron job is a task scheduled to run automatically at fixed times or intervals, defined using cron's time-based syntax (minute, hour, day, month, weekday) on Linux systems.
What is SSH?: SSH (Secure Shell) is an encrypted network protocol used to securely log into and execute commands on a remote machine, and is the transport Ansible and most server administration relies on.
What is the difference between a process: A process is an independently running program with its own isolated memory space; a thread is a lighter-weight unit of execution within a process that shares that process's memory with other threads, making threads cheaper to create but requiring careful synchronization.
What is a zombie process, and how do you: A zombie is a process that has already finished executing but still has an entry in the process table (shown as state Z) because its parent hasn't yet called wait() to read its exit status - it holds no real resources beyond that table entry, so one zombie is harmless. A large number of zombies means the parent process has a bug (not reaping its children); the real fix is fixing or restarting that parent process, since you cannot kill a zombie directly (it's already dead) - killing/fixing the parent lets the init/systemd process adopt and reap any orphaned zombies.
What is a file descriptor limit (ulimit): Every open file, socket, or pipe consumes a file descriptor, and Linux caps how many a process can hold open via ulimit - a soft limit (what's actually enforced, raisable by the user up to the hard limit) and a hard limit (the ceiling, raisable only by root). A 'too many open files' error means the process hit its soft limit; fix it by raising the limit in /etc/security/limits.conf, the systemd unit's LimitNOFILE setting, or ulimit -n in the shell - but also check whether the application is leaking file handles/connections instead of closing them, since raising the limit alone just delays the same failure at a higher ceiling.
What is CPU throttling?: CPU throttling happens when a process's CPU usage is capped - deliberately, by a container CPU limit, or by hardware thermal limits - and it's forced to run slower or is paused once it exceeds its allotted CPU time in a given period.
What is a message queue?: A message queue is a middleware component (like Pub/Sub, RabbitMQ, or Kafka) that decouples producers and consumers by buffering messages between them, enabling asynchronous processing and absorbing load spikes.
What is caching?: Caching stores frequently accessed data in a faster-to-read location (memory, CDN, local disk) so subsequent requests can be served without recomputing or re-fetching from the slower original source.
What is a monolith versus a microservice: A monolith is a single deployable application containing all functionality in one codebase and process; a microservices architecture splits functionality into many independently deployable services that communicate over the network, trading simplicity for independent scaling and deployment.
What is a typical CI/CD pipeline flow, s: A typical flow is: checkout source code, install dependencies, run static analysis/lint, run unit tests, build an artifact (binary or container image), run integration tests, push the artifact to a registry, deploy to a staging environment, run smoke/acceptance tests, then promote (often with manual approval) to production, followed by post-deploy verification.
What is a Jenkins pipeline?: A Jenkins pipeline is a suite of automated build/test/deploy steps defined as code (in a Jenkinsfile) that Jenkins executes as a single connected workflow, giving you version-controlled, repeatable CI/CD instead of clicking through freestyle job configuration in the UI.
What is the difference between a Jenkins: A declarative pipeline uses a fixed, structured syntax (pipeline { stages { stage { steps { ... } } } }) that's easier to read and validate but less flexible; a scripted pipeline is written in full Groovy inside a node {} block, giving complete programmatic control at the cost of more complexity - declarative is the recommended default for most teams today.
What is a Jenkinsfile?: A Jenkinsfile is a text file, checked into source control alongside the application code, that defines a Jenkins pipeline's stages and steps as code, so the build/test/deploy process is versioned and reviewable like any other code change.
What is a Jenkins agent (node)?: An agent (formerly called a 'node' or 'slave') is a machine - physical, VM, or container - that Jenkins connects to and dispatches build work to, letting the controller (master) distribute pipeline execution across multiple workers rather than running everything on one machine.
What are Jenkins plugins, and can you na: Plugins extend Jenkins with integrations and features beyond its core - common ones include Git/GitHub plugin (source checkout), Pipeline plugin (Jenkinsfile support), Docker plugin (build/run containers), Credentials Binding (secret management), Blue Ocean (pipeline visualization), and Kubernetes plugin (dynamic agent provisioning on a cluster).
What is GitLab CI/CD?: GitLab CI/CD is GitLab's built-in continuous integration and delivery system, where pipelines are defined in a .gitlab-ci.yml file at the repo root and executed by GitLab Runners whenever code is pushed or merged.
What is a .gitlab-ci.yml file?: The .gitlab-ci.yml file is a YAML file at the root of a GitLab repository that defines the pipeline's stages and jobs - what to run, in what order, on what triggers, and with what scripts - which GitLab automatically picks up and executes.
What is a GitLab CI/CD runner?: A GitLab Runner is the agent process that actually executes the jobs defined in .gitlab-ci.yml - it can be shared (GitLab-hosted) or self-hosted, and picks up queued jobs from GitLab and reports results back.
What is GitHub Actions?: GitHub Actions is GitHub's native CI/CD and automation platform, where workflows defined in YAML files under .github/workflows/ run in response to repository events (push, pull request, schedule, etc.) on GitHub-hosted or self-hosted runners.
What is a GitHub Actions workflow file?: A workflow file is a YAML file under .github/workflows/ that defines triggers (on:), jobs, and steps - each step typically either runs a shell command or invokes a reusable 'Action' from the marketplace or another repo.
What is a GitHub Actions runner?: A runner is the server (GitHub-hosted or self-hosted) that executes a workflow's jobs - GitHub-hosted runners are ephemeral VMs provisioned per job, while self-hosted runners are your own persistent machines registered to a repo or org.
What is the difference between Jenkins,: Jenkins is a self-hosted, plugin-driven automation server that works with any Git provider and needs its own infrastructure to run and maintain; GitLab CI/CD is tightly integrated into GitLab itself (pipeline defined in .gitlab-ci.yml, runs via GitLab Runners); GitHub Actions is GitHub's native equivalent (workflows in .github/workflows/, runs on GitHub-hosted or self-hosted runners) - Jenkins offers the most flexibility and plugin ecosystem, while GitLab CI and GitHub Actions offer simpler setup since they're built into the platform you're already using for source control.
